content:
{
  "title": "Prompt-Scoped Feedback: Iterative, In-Context Error Correction for Enhanced Code Generation",
  "problem": "Current LLM code generation performance plateaus with few-shot prompting and struggles particularly with multi-step or compositional coding tasks. Most prompting strategies do not incorporate real-time feedback or error signals within a single inference window.",
  "motivation": "Despite strong advances, code LLMs lag behind expert programmers, especially on complex tasks. Recent work suggests post-hoc error correction or chain-of-thought can help, but these require multiple calls or partial supervision. A method to harness model self-critique and correction in a single prompt could dramatically boost sample quality and efficiency, especially as models scale.",
  "method": "We propose 'Prompt-Scoped Feedback' (PSF), a novel prompting scheme where the model first generates candidate code, then—in the same prompt—is asked to analyze its own output for likely runtime or logic errors, and finally to regenerate the code incorporating its own critique. Critique instructions are targeted to common code error classes, guiding the model to simulate a code review cycle internally. The prompt template chains generation, self-critique, and revision without any external tools or multiple API calls. This method, unlike chain-of-thought, explicitly focuses the model on error-driven self-improvement within one inference.",
  "experiment_plan": "Evaluate PSF on HumanEval, MBPP, and APPS datasets, comparing to standard zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought prompts. Baselines include state-of-the-art prompting and post-hoc self-correction. Metrics are pass@k, functional correctness, and error class reduction rates. Ablate critique specificity and ordering to isolate effects."
}
